survival_reason: The mutant survives because the mutation changes which thread writes the warp_sum to shared memory, but due to warp-level execution semantics and the subsequent reduction pattern, the final result remains identical. In the original, only thread 0 of each warp writes warp_sum to block_sums[warp.meta_group_rank()]. In the mutant, threads 1-31 write the same warp_sum value (identical across all threads in the warp after reduction) to the same location. This creates a benign race where all writing threads store identical values. During the subsequent reduction in the first warp, the reading threads (threads 0-31 of warp 0) load from block_sums, which contains the same values regardless of which threads wrote them, since warp_sum is uniform per warp. The mutation is functionally equivalent for all inputs with the given shape constraints.
kill_strategy: No input can expose a difference because: (1) warp_sum is identical for all threads in a warp after the warp reduction (line 53), (2) all writing threads target the same shared memory location per warp, storing identical values, (3) the reading phase only depends on the stored values, not which threads wrote them. Even with extreme values (NaN, inf, zero), the warp reduction produces identical warp_sum across threads, making the write race benign. Different input shapes/dimensions cannot help because the bug is in per-warp coordination, and the execution pattern remains identical across warps.
recommendations: The kernel should be hardened against such mutations by using atomic operations or explicit thread synchronization for shared memory writes, though performance may degrade. Alternatively, the test suite should include varying thread block dimensions and grid sizes to expose shape-dependent bugs. Currently, tests fix the input shape, missing mutations that affect warp/block partitioning. Adding tests with non-power-of-two sizes, small tensors (fewer threads than warp size), and different block dimensions could improve coverage for coordination bugs.

robustness_suggestion: The kernel has a benign race condition where multiple threads in a warp can write identical values to the same shared memory location. While harmless in this specific case (due to warp-uniform values), such patterns are fragile and violate CUDA's memory model. Defensive coding should enforce single-threaded writes to shared memory to prevent undefined behavior when values diverge. Use explicit synchronization and designated writer threads to guarantee correctness across all execution scenarios.
